revision.text:
[[ja:空の神兵]] [[File:IJA_Paratroopers_Teishin_Shudan_in_Palembang_Feb_1942.png|thumb|right|250px|Imperial Japanese Army paratroopers (Teishin Shudan), known as the "Divine Soldiers of the Sky."]] '''Divine Soldiers of the Sky''' (空の神兵, ''Sora no Shinpei'') refers to the paratrooper units (airborne units or Teishin Shudan) of the {{ill|Imperial Japanese Army|en|Imperial Japanese Army|qid=Q276535|lt=Imperial Japanese Army|1=Imperial Japanese Army}} and {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|en|Imperial Japanese Navy|qid=Q219712|lt=Imperial Japanese Navy|1=Imperial Japanese Navy}} during World War II. The term also refers to a Japanese [[:en:military song|military song]] (wartime kakyoku) dedicated to these units and a 1942 film depicting the Army paratroopers. --- == Japanese Paratrooper Units == [[File:IJA-1st-Yokosuka-Force-Paratroopers-Manado-Langoan-Airfield.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Paratroopers descending on Palembang with their white parachutes, referred to as "white roses of the sky."]] {{main|Teishin Shudan|1st Raiding Group|Imperial Japanese Navy Paratroopers|qq=}} During the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} (''Greater East Asia War''), the {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|en|Imperial Japanese Navy|qid=Q219712|lt=Navy|1=Imperial Japanese Navy}}’s [[Yokosuka 1st Special Naval Landing Force]] conducted a surprise attack and parachute drop on {{ill|Manado|en|Manado|qid=Q15847|lt=Manado|1=Manado}} (Menado) in {{ill|Sulawesi|en|Sulawesi|qid=Q3812|lt=Celebes Island|1=Sulawesi}} on January 11, 1942. Similarly, on February 14, 1942, the Army's [[1st Raiding Group]] (''Teishin Shudan'') launched an airborne assault on {{ill|Palembang|en|Palembang|qid=Q8131|lt=Palembang|1=Palembang}} in {{ill|Sumatra|en|Sumatra|qid=Q3492|lt=Sumatra|1=Sumatra}} as part of the [[Dutch East Indies campaign#Palembang Airborne Operation|Palembang Airborne Operation]]. The successful operations, which targeted enemy-held airfields (Manado and Palembang), oilfields, and refineries (both at Palembang), earned these units the affectionate moniker "Divine Soldiers of the Sky" (空の神兵). --- == Military Song: "Divine Soldiers of the Sky" == {{External media |topic=Listen to full versions |audio1=[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pOc0efmDgR0 "Divine Soldiers of the Sky"] (sung by [[Narumitsu Shinsuke]] and [[Shika Fumiko]]) |audio2=[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zOmdqWT_qhU "Divine Soldiers of the Sky"] (sung by [[:en:Haida Katsuhiko|Haida Katsuhiko]] and [[Ōtani Retsuko]])
Provided by [[:en:JVC Kenwood|JVC Kenwood]] on YouTube|qq=}} * Lyrics: [[Umeki Saburō]] * Composer: [[Takagi Tōroku]] * Performers: Narumitsu Shinsuke and Shika Fumiko / Haida Katsuhiko and Ōtani Retsuko This military song, dedicated to Japanese paratrooper units, was released by [[:en:Victor Records|Victor Records]] in April 1942. The lyrics liken descending parachutes to "white roses blooming in the blue sky," incorporating vivid imagery of blue skies, white parachutes, and red blood. The melody is unique for a military song, featuring an almost waltz-like rhythm, and includes evocative chimes and glockenspiel in the interlude, symbolizing parachutes. The composer, Takagi Tōroku, commented on the song in conflicting accounts: in one, he expressed regret about the militaristic context of his work, while in another, he admired the poetic and aesthetic qualities of the lyrics that inspired him to complete the composition within 15 minutes.See ''Shokun!'' magazine, September 2001 edition, for an interview with author-director [[Kuze Mitsuhiko]]. The song sold 33,000 records between August 1943 and August 1944.''Ongaku Bunka'' magazine, November 1944 issue, cited in [[Yoshihiro Kurata]], ''The History of Japanese Record Culture'' (Tokyo Shoseki, 1992). The song was also used as the theme for the [[#Film "Divine Soldiers of the Sky"|film]] about Imperial Japanese Army paratroopers. --- == Film: "Divine Soldiers of the Sky" {{Infobox Film |title=Divine Soldiers of the Sky |director=[[Watanabe Yoshimi]] |producer=Imperial Japanese Army Air Corps |screenwriter=Watanabe Yoshimi |cinematography=[[Kawaguchi Kazuo]] and [[Fukuda Saburō]] |music=[[Takagi Tōroku]] |theme_song="Divine Soldiers of the Sky" |released={{flagicon|Japan|qq=}} September 1942 |runtime=55 minutes |language=Japanese }} == The 1942 film directed by Watanabe Yoshimi was a {{ill|Propaganda|en|Propaganda|qid=Q7281|lt=Propaganda|1=Propaganda}} documentary showcasing the rigorous training and operations of the Imperial Japanese Army’s paratroopers (Teishin Shudan). The film follows recruits transforming into elite paratroopers, highlighting their physical training, equipment handling, and airborne deployment exercises. Authentic equipment such as [[Type 97 transport aircraft]], [[Type 94 pistols]], [[Type 99 rifles]], and [[Type 92 heavy machine guns]] are depicted. The theme song [[#Military Song|''Divine Soldiers of the Sky'']] features prominently in both vocal and instrumental versions. The film was later re-released on DVD in 2004 by [[Nihon Eiga Shinsha]] (DVD code DNN-684). --- == Legacy in the Japan Ground Self-Defense Force == The song "Divine Soldiers of the Sky" has been adopted by the {{ill|Japan Ground Self-Defense Force|en|Japan Ground Self-Defense Force|qid=Q889751|lt=Japan Ground Self-Defense Force|1=Japan Ground Self-Defense Force}}’s [[:en:1st Airborne Brigade|1st Airborne Brigade]], regarded as the successor to the Imperial Army's paratrooper units. Established by [[Ikegasa Susuo]] and staffed by former wartime paratroopers, the brigade continues the traditions of the original paratroopers. The song is performed during key events like "First Drop Training" and at exhibitions such as the [[Fuji Firepower Demonstration]]. --- == See Also == * [[The Kato Hayabusa Squadron (film)]] * [[Momotarō: Sacred Sailors]] * [[Tsuruta Gorō]]'s war painting "The Divine Soldiers Descend on Palembang" (Tokyo National Museum of Modern Art). {{translated page|ja|空の神兵|version=104405928|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:空の神兵]] {{wikidata link|Q11597341}} [[Category:1942 films]] [[Category:1942 songs]] [[Category:1st Airborne Brigade (Japan)]] [[Category:Compositions by Tōroku Takagi]] [[Category:Imperial Japanese Army]] [[Category:Imperial Japanese Navy]] [[Category:Japanese documentary films]] [[Category:Japanese Military Songs]] [[Category:Japanese War Films before 1945]] [[Category:Songs starting with So]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
